# 1. Learning Outcome

-   Basic principles and essential components of **`GGPLOT2`**.
-   Deep dive on each of the "Grammar of Graphics" building blocks.
-   Apply essential graphical elements provided by **`GGPLOT2`** to create elegant and functional statistical graphics.

![](images/ho_ex01_poster.png)

# 2. Data Preparation

::::: panel-tabset
## Installing and loading libraries

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

The code chunk below uses `p_load()`, a [pacman](https://rpubs.com/akshaypatankar/594834) package to check if the tidyverse library is installed in the computer.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pacman::p_load(tidyverse)
```
:::


## Importing data

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

-   The code chunk below imports *exam_data.csv* into the R environment by using [*read_csv()*](https://readr.tidyverse.org/reference/read_delim.html) function of [**readr**](https://readr.tidyverse.org/) package.
-   **readr** is one of the tidyverse package.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")
```
:::

:::::

::::: panel-tabset
## Data summary

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Overview

The summary shows a year-end examination grades. There are 7 features:

-   Categorical: `ID`, `CLASS`, `GENDER`, `RACE`
-   Continuous: `MATH`, `ENGLISH`, `SCIENCE`
:::


::: {.cell}

:::


## Data preview

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Overview

These are the top rows of exam_data
:::


::: {.cell}

:::

:::::

# 3. Discovery

## A layered grammar of graphics

Figure below shows the seven grammars of **`GGPLOT2`**.

![](https://blog.gramener.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/11/7-layers-of-grammar-of-graphics-to-tell-powerful-data-stories-3.png)

-   **`Themes`**: modify all non-data components of a plot, such as main title, sub-title, y-aixs title, or legend background.
-   **`Coordinates`**: define the plane on which data are mapped on the graphic.
-   **`Statistics`**: Statistical transformations that summarise data (e.g. mean, confidence intervals).
-   **`Facets`**: split the data into subsets to create multiple variations of the same graph (paneling, multiple plots).
-   **`Geometries`**: The visual elements used for our data, such as point, bar or line.
-   **`Aesthetics`**: Take attributes of the data and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, colors, size, shape, or transparency.
-   **`Data`**: The dataset being plotted.

## GGPLOT2 introduction

**`GGPLOT2`** is an R package for creating data-driven graphics based on the "Grammar of Graphics". It is also part of the tidyverse family specially designed for visual exploration and communication. For more detail, visit [ggplot2 link](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/).

## R Graphics VS **`GGPLOT2`**

Comparison between R graphics and **`GGPLOT2`**

::: panel-tabset
## R Graphics


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
hist(exam_data$MATHS, 
     ylab='Number of Students', 
     xlab='Score', 
     main='Distribution of Maths scores',
     col='#FFC107')
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## GGPLOT2


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=10, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="black", 
                 fill="#FFC107") +
  labs(x = "Score",
       y = "Number of Students",
       title = "Distribution of Maths scores")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

:::

### Why is **`GGPLOT2`** recommended?

> The transferable skills from ggplot2 are not the idiosyncrasies of plotting syntax, but a powerful way of thinking about visualisation, as a way of mapping between variables and the visual properties of geometric objects that you can perceive. \~ [Hadley Wickham](http://varianceexplained.org/r/teach_ggplot2_to_beginners/#comment-1745406157)

# 4. Grammatical Element: DATA

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Overview

Let us call the ggplot() function using the code chunk below.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

-   A blank canvas appears.

-   ggplot() initializes a ggplot object.

-   The data argument defines the dataset to be used for plotting.

-   If the dataset is not already a data.frame, it will be converted to one by fortify().
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# 5. Grammatical Element: AESTHETIC

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Overview

The aesthetic mappings take attributes of the data and and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, color, size, shape, or transparency. Each visual characteristic can thus encode an aspect of the data and be used to convey information.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

-   All aesthetics of a plot are specified in the [`aes()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/aes.html) function call.

-   Code chunk below adds the aesthetic element into the plot.

-   ggplot2 includes the x-axis and the axis’s label.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=MATHS))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# 6. Grammatical Element: GEOMETRIES

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Overview

Geometric objects are the actual marks we put on a plot, in this exercise, I will showcase:

-   **`geom_bar`**: Creates bar charts to visualize the frequency, count, or proportion of categorical variables.

-   **`geom_dotplot`**: Represents individual data points as stacked dots to show the exact distribution of small, continuous datasets.

-   **`geom_histogram`**: Groups continuous data into connected vertical bins to display its underlying frequency distribution.

-   **`geom_density`**: Creates a smoothed, continuous curve to estimate the probability distribution of a numeric variable.

-   **`geom_boxplot`**: Summarizes the spread of continuous data using its median, quartiles, and potential statistical outliers.

-   **`geom_violin`**: Combines a boxplot and a density plot to reveal the full shape, width, and peaks of a data distribution.

-   **`geom_point`**: Creates scatter plots by plotting individual coordinates to display the relationship between two continuous variables.
:::

::: callout-tip
-   A plot must have at least one geom; there is no upper limit. You can add a geom to a plot using the + operator.

-   For complete list, please refer to [here](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/#section-layer-geoms).
:::

## 6.1 BASIC GEOMETRIES

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Object: geom_bar

The code chunk below plots a bar chart by using [`geom_bar()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/geom_bar.html).
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Object: geom_dotplot

In a dot plot, the width of a dot corresponds to the bin width (or maximum width, depending on the binning algorithm), and dots are stacked, with each dot representing one observation.
:::

::::: panel-tabset
## geom_dotplot()

::: callout-caution
-   The ***y*** scale is not very useful, in fact it is very misleading.
-   **`binwidth`** defaults to 1/30 of the range of the data.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(dotsize = 0.5)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## geom_dotplot() - *modified*

::: callout-note
## Fix

The code chunk below performs the following two steps:

-   `scale_y_continuous()` is used to turn off the y-axis, and - **`binwidth`** argument is used to change the binwidth to 2.5.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth=2.5,         
               dotsize = 0.5) +      
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,           
                     breaks = NULL)  
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-11-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

:::::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Object: geom_histogram

In the code chunk below, [*geom_histogram()*](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/geom_histogram.html) is used to create a simple histogram by using values in *MATHS* field of *exam_data*.
:::

::::: panel-tabset
## geom_histogram()

::: callout-caution
Note that the default bin is **30**.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram()       
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## geom_histogram() - *modified*

::: callout-note
## Fix

For grades, a **`binwidth`** of 5 or 10 is the standard "human logic" approach.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5)       
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-13-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

:::::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Object: geom_point

[`geom_point()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/geom_point.html) is especially useful for creating scatterplot.
:::

::::: panel-tabset
## geom_point()

::: callout-note
## Configuration

The code chunk below plots a scatterplot showing the Maths and English grades of pupils by using `geom_point()`, grouped by gender.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= ENGLISH,
           color = GENDER)) +
  geom_point()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-14-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## geom_point() - *modified*

::: callout-note
## Configuration

To add reference lines:
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= ENGLISH, 
           y = MATHS, 
           color = GENDER)) +
  geom_point() +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = 50),
             col = 'grey',
             linewidth = 0.5,
             linetype = "dashed") + 
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = 50),
             col = 'grey',
             linewidth = 0.5,
             linetype = "dashed") +  
  labs(x = "English Score",
       y = "Math Score",
       title = "Math Score against English Score by Gender")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

:::::

:::::: callout-note
## Object: geom_boxplot

[`geom_boxplot()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/geom_boxplot.html) displays continuous value list. It visualises five summary statistics (the median, two hinges and two whiskers), and all “outlying” points individually.

::::: panel-tabset
## original


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS,       
           x= GENDER)) +    
  geom_boxplot()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-16-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## notches

::: callout-note
## Configuration

[Notches](https://sites.google.com/site/davidsstatistics/home/notched-box-plots) are used in box plots to help visually assess whether the medians of distributions differ. If the notches do not overlap, this is evidence that the medians are different.

The code chunk below plots the distribution of Maths scores by gender in notched plot instead of boxplot.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot(notch=TRUE)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## facet_wrap

::: callout-note
## Configuration

We can also incorporate facet_wrap() to create boxplots of math scores for each class that is separated by gender.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x = CLASS)) +
  geom_boxplot() + 
  facet_wrap(~ GENDER)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-18-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

:::::
::::::

## 6.2 ADVANCE GEOMETRIES

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Object: geom_hex

-   **Usage**: It divides the plot into regular hexagons and colors each hexagon based on the number of points (density) within it.
-   **Best for**: Visualizing the relationship between two continuous variables when you have a large dataset (thousands of points).
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, y = CLASS)) + geom_hex()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-19-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Object: geom_density

-   **Usage**: Use this when you want to see the underlying shape of the data without the jagged edges of histogram bins.
-   **Best for**: Visualizing the distribution of a single continuous variable.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) + geom_density()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-20-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Object: geom_violin

-   **Usage**: It is essentially a mirrored version of a density plot, rotated 90 degrees and displayed like a boxplot.
-   **Best for**: Comparing the distribution of a continuous variable across different categories (one discrete, one continuous).
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS, y = GENDER)) + geom_violin()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-21-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## GEOMETRIC MODIFICATIONS

::::: panel-tabset
## with geom()

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

-   bins argument is used to change the number of bins,

-   fill argument is used to shade the histogram, and

-   color argument is used to change the outline colour of the bars
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20,            
                 color="black",      
                 fill="#FFC107")  
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-22-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## with aes()

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

-   The code chunk below changes the interior color of the histogram (i.e. fill) by using sub-group of aesthetic().

-   This approach can be used to color or fill the alpha of the geometric.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 color="grey30")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-23-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

:::::

:::: callout-note
## COMBINATION OF OBJECTS

The code chunks below plots the data points on the boxplots by using both `geom_boxplot()` and `geom_point()`.

::: callout-important
The order of geom layer matters! Check code chunks below.
:::
::::

::::: panel-tabset
## Combination

::: callout-note
## Configuration

geom_point() before geom_boxplot()
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +

  geom_point(position = 'jitter',
             size=0.5) +
  geom_boxplot()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-24-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Combination - Modified

::: callout-note
## Configuration

geom_boxplot() before geom_point()
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +                    
  geom_point(position="jitter", 
             size = 0.5)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-25-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

:::::

# 7. Grammatical Element: FACETS

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Overview

Facetting generates small multiples (sometimes also called trellis plot), each displaying a different subset of the data. They are an alternative to aesthetics for displaying additional discrete variables. ggplot2 supports two types of factes, namely: [`facet_wrap`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/facet_wrap.html) and [`facet_grid()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/facet_grid.html).
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration - facet_wrap()

The code chunk below plots a trellis plot using `facet_wrap()`.
:::


::: {.cell}

:::


::: callout-note
## Configuration - facet_wrap() with extras

Separation by Color, combined by gender (using "fill" Aesthetic)
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = exam_data,        
       aes(x = MATHS, 
           fill = GENDER)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, 
                 position = "dodge",
                 color = "black") + 
  facet_wrap(~ CLASS)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-27-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


::: callout-note
## Configuration - facet_wrap() with extras

Separation by Gender (using the "facet_grid" feature)
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(data = exam_data,        
       aes(x = MATHS)) +  
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, 
                 color = "black") +    
  facet_grid(GENDER ~ CLASS)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-28-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## 7.1 FUNCTION: FACET_WRAP()

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Overview

[`facet_wrap`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/facet_wrap.html) wraps a 1d sequence of panels into 2d. This is generally a better use of screen space than `facet_grid` because most displays are roughly rectangular.
:::

:::::: panel-tabset
## Subsetting data

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

If we only want to visualise classes 3A, 3B, 3C, 3D, we could subset the exam data to include only these students using `filter()` from the **dplyr** package.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
sub_data <- exam_data %>% 
  filter(CLASS %in% c('3A', '3B', '3C', '3D'))

ggplot(data=sub_data,
       aes(x=MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
  facet_wrap(~CLASS) +
  labs(y='Number of students', 
       x='Math scores',
       title = "Maths scores for Classes 3A, 3B, 3C, 3D")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-29-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Multiple overlay of Histograms

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

We could also overlay the genders if we want to.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=MATHS, fill = GENDER)) + #Add fill to split histogram categories
  geom_histogram(position = "identity", alpha=0.2, bins=20) +
  labs(y='Number of students', 
       x='Math scores',
       title = "Maths scores by Gender")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-30-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## Grouped data

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

Two separate graphs but using the same grouped data.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data = exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, 
           x = CLASS)) +
  geom_boxplot() + 
  facet_wrap(~ GENDER)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-31-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

::::::

## 7.2 FUNCTION: FACET_GRID()

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Overview

[`facet_grid()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/facet_grid.html) forms a matrix of panels defined by row and column facetting variables. It is most useful when you have two discrete variables, and all combinations of the variables exist in the data.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

The code chunk below plots a trellis plot using [`facet_grid()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/facet_grid.html).
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20) +
  facet_grid(~CLASS) +
  labs(y='Number of students', 
       x='Math scores',
       title = "Maths scores by Class")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-32-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# 8. Grammatical Element: STATISTICS

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Overview

The [Statistics functions](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/#stats) statistically transform data, usually as some form of summary. For example:

-   frequency of values of a variable (bar graph)

    -   a mean

    -   a confidence limit

-   There are two ways to use these functions:

    -   add a `stat_()` function and override the default geom, or

    -   add a `geom_()` function and override the default stat.
:::

## 8.1 Using STAT Function

::::: panel-tabset
## stat()

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

The boxplots below are incomplete because the positions of the means were not shown.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes( y= MATHS, x = GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-33-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## stat_summary()

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

The code chunk below **adds mean values** by using [`stat_summary()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/stat_summary.html) function and overriding the default geom.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  stat_summary(geom = 'point',
               fun.y='mean',
               colour = 'red',
               size=4)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-34-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

:::::

## 8.2 Using GEOM Function

::::: panel-tabset
## geom()

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

The code chunk below adding mean values by using `geom_()` function and overriding the default stat.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(y = MATHS, x= GENDER)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  geom_point(stat="summary",        
             fun.y="mean",           
             colour ="red",          
             size=4)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-35-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## geom_smooth()

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

In the code chunk below, [`geom_smooth()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/geom_smooth.html) is used to plot a best fit curve on the scatterplot.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(size=1)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-36-1.png){width=672}

:::::

## 8.3 Adding a best fit curve

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Overview

The default method used in [`geom_smooth()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/geom_smooth.html)is `loess`.
:::

:::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
The default smoothing method can be overridden as shown below.
:::
::::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(size=1, method = lm)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-37-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# 9. Grammatical Element: COORDINATES

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Overview

The *Coordinates* functions map the position of objects onto the plane of the plot. There are a number of different possible coordinate systems to use, they are:

-   [`coord_cartesian()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/coord_cartesian.html): the default cartesian coordinate systems, where you specify x and y values (e.g. allows you to zoom in or out).

-   [`coord_flip()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/coord_flip.html): a cartesian system with the x and y flipped.

-   [`coord_fixed()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/coord_fixed.html): a cartesian system with a “fixed” aspect ratio (e.g. 1.78 for a “widescreen” plot).

-   [`coord_quickmap()`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/coord_map.html): a coordinate system that approximates a good aspect ratio for maps.
:::

## 9.1 coord_flip()

::::: panel-tabset
## vertical (default)

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

Bar chart of ggplot2 is in vertical form by default.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar()+
    labs(y='Number of students', 
       x='Race',
       title = "Number of Students by Race")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-38-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## horizontal

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

The code chunk below flips the horizontal bar chart into vertical bar chart by using `coord_flip()`.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
    labs(y='Number of students', 
       x='Race',
       title = "Number of Students by Race")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-39-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

:::::

## 9.2 coord_cartesian

::::: panel-tabset
## default

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

The scatterplot below is slightly misleading because the y-aixs and x-axis range are not equal.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +
      labs(y='English', 
       x='Maths',
       title = "Graph of English against Math scores")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-40-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## edited

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

The code chunk below fixed both the y-axis and x-axis range from 0-100.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5) +
  coord_cartesian (xlim=c(0,100),
                   ylim= c(0,100))+
        labs(y='English', 
       x='Maths',
       title = "Graph of English against Math scores")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-41-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

:::::

# 10. Grammatical Element: THEMES

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Overview

Themes control elements of the graph not related to the data such as background colour, font size, gridlines, and colour of labels.

Built-in themes include `theme_gray()` (default), `theme_bw()`, `theme_classic()`

A list of theme can be found at this [link](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/ggtheme.html). Each theme element can be conceived of as either a line (e.g. x-axis), a rectangle (e.g. graph background), or text (e.g. axis title).
:::

## 10.1 Built-in themes

::::: panel-tabset
## theme_minimal()

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

A horizontal bar chart plotted using `theme_minimal()`.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-42-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## theme_classic()

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

A horizontal bar chart plotted using `theme_classic()`.

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_classic()
```

:::::

## 10.2 Special built-in Themes

::::: panel-tabset
## theme_void()

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

Removes all non-data elements (axes, labels, grid). This is technically the most "advanced" use case as it is required for non-standard plots like maps, pie charts, or network diagrams.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_void()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-44-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## theme_test()

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Configuration

A stable, bare-bones theme primarily used by developers for visual unit testing to ensure new features don't break existing layouts.
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show the code"}
ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x=RACE)) +
  geom_bar() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_test()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](HO_EX01_new_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-45-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::
